channel.title: NYT Book Reviews (Filtered)
channel.description: Actual book reviews from the New York Times, filtered from the noisy Books RSS feed.

item.title: ‘Villa Coco,’ by Andrew Sean Greer
item.description: Following his “Less” books with “Villa Coco,” Andrew Sean Greer drops an aimless postgraduate into a glamorous, romantic and secret-laden setting.
item.author: Stuart Emmrich

item.title: ‘The Man Who Stole the Gods,’ by Matthew Campbell
item.description: In “The Man Who Stole the Gods,” Matthew Campbell recounts a shocking, decades-long crime and the search for its perpetrator.
item.author: Hugh Eakin

item.title: ‘Checkmate,’ by Ben Mezrich
item.description: The story at the heart of “Checkmate” is a good one, but Ben Mezrich has hustled it into print too quickly.
item.author: Dylan Loeb McClain

item.title: ‘The Traveler,’ by Andrea Wulf
item.description: As presented in Andrea Wulf’s new biography, “The Traveler,” George Forster was an impressively curious, open-minded 17-year-old naturalist and polymath.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Wreck of the Mentor,’ by Eric Jay Dolin
item.description: In “The Wreck of the Mentor,” the maritime historian Eric Jay Dolin brings to life a dramatic episode from the golden age of whaling.
item.author: Blair Braverman

item.title: ‘Stolen Revolution,’ by Yeganeh Torbati and Bozorgmehr Sharafedin
item.description: In a quietly devastating new book, two journalists chart the protest movements fighting for change inside the country.
item.author: Reza Aslan

item.title: ‘What Came West,’ by Josh Weil
item.description: Josh Weil’s new novel follows an autistic trapper on an odyssey during the California gold rush.
item.author: Hamilton Cain

item.title: ‘Land,’ by Maggie O’Farrell
item.description: Set in the decades after the Great Hunger, “Land” is a rich portrait of family life amid Ireland’s long struggle against British rule.
item.author: Fiona Mozley

item.title: ‘The Fire Agent,’ by David Baerwald
item.description: “The Fire Agent,” by David Baerwald, is a historical novel that spans two continents and world wars.
item.author: Dan Fesperman

item.title: ‘Whistler,’ by Ann Patchett
item.description: In “Whistler,” a surprise encounter at the Met changes the course of their lives.
item.author: Helen Schulman

item.title: ‘The Man Who Read Everything: The Literary Letters of Harold Bloom’
item.description: A collection of Harold Bloom’s letters details the working life of one of America’s most influential intellects.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘1873,’ by Liaquat Ahamed
item.description: In “1873,” the historian and financier Liaquat Ahamed traces the political consequences of booming markets that left a lot of people behind.
item.author: Trevor Jackson

item.title: ‘Rabbit, Fox, Tar,’ by P.C. Verrone
item.description: In “Rabbit, Fox, Tar,” a white neighborhood’s local election is complicated when a mysterious, dark-skinned woman suddenly appears in town.
item.author: Mateo Askaripour

item.title: ‘Girl’s Girl,’ by Sonia Feldman
item.description: In Sonia Feldman’s novel, “Girl’s Girl,” the delicate balance of a Gen Z friend group is unsettled over one Ohio summer.
item.author: Sanjena Sathian

item.title: ‘Sublimation,’ by Isabel J. Kim
item.description: In her book “Sublimation,” Isabel J. Kim reimagines the dilemmas of immigration through a science fiction story about scheming clones.
item.author: Daniel Nieh

item.title: ‘View From the East Wing,’ by Jill Biden
item.description: Beyond a few pointed digs at her husband’s successor, “View From the East Wing” largely sticks to the head-spinning details of first lady-hood.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Found Sound,’ by Meg Wolitzer and Charlie Panek, and ‘Stream,’ by Aida Salazar
item.description: Meg Wolitzer and Charlie Panek’s “Found Sound” and Aida Salazar’s “Stream” send their protagonists on a listening (and healing) tour of real life.
item.author: Jennifer Howard

item.title: ‘Stalin’s Apostles,’ by Antonia Senior
item.description: A new book by Antonia Senior chronicles the careers of five men who betrayed their country — with devastating results.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Midnight Train,’ by Matt Haig
item.description: Matt Haig returns with another life-affirming novel, this one about a man on a magical train that helps him revisit key moments of his life.
item.author: Elisabeth Egan

item.title: ‘Freedom Round the Globe,’ by Sarah M.S. Pearsall
item.description: In “Freedom Round the Globe,” Sarah M.S. Pearsall tracks the stirrings of liberty beyond the British colonies that became the United States.
item.author: Jon Meacham

item.title: ‘Babylon, South Dakota,’ by Tom Lin
item.description: Tom Lin’s new novel, “Babylon, South Dakota,” reimagines the western with surreal elements.
item.author: Marcel Theroux

item.title: ‘The Vivisectors,’ by Missouri Williams
item.description: The stoic narrator of “The Vivisectors” befriends a student embroiled in a cultural politics debate.
item.author: Ann Manov

item.title: ‘Trudeau & Doonesbury,’ by Joshua Kendall
item.description: A new biography of Garry Trudeau tracks the rise of a comic strip that brought counterculture and political opinions to the funny pages.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘America, U.S.A.,’ by Eddie S. Glaude Jr.
item.description: In “America, U.S.A.,” Eddie Glaude Jr. looks back at the country’s past anniversaries with skepticism and pain.
item.author: Richard Kreitner

item.title: ‘The Danger to Be Sane,’ by Rosa Montero
item.description: In “The Danger to Be Sane,” the journalist Rosa Montero delves into the connections between psychic turbulence and creative work — including her own.
item.author: Carmela Ciuraru

item.title: ‘The Land and Its People,’ by David Sedaris
item.description: In David Sedaris’s wry new collection, being alive is as weird, atrocious, contradictory, unfair and funny as ever.
item.author: Roddy Doyle

item.title: ‘Dekonstructing the Kardashians,’ by MJ Corey
item.description: In “Dekonstructing the Kardashians,” MJ Corey takes America’s most famous TV clan seriously.
item.author: Jessica Bennett

item.title: ‘Dog Days,’ by Emily LaBarge
item.description: In her ingenious “Dog Days,” Emily LaBarge writes about a terrifying event without resorting to “the trauma plot.”
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Glyph,’ by Ali Smith
item.description: Ali Smith’s new novel, “Glyph,” dispenses with subtleties and takes on an age of war and the ghosts left behind.
item.author: Megan O’Grady

item.title: ‘Keeper of My Kin,’ by Ada Ferrer
item.description: A stirring memoir by the Pulitzer Prize-winning historian Ada Ferrer chronicles her family’s life in the aftermath of traumatic rupture.
item.author: Miguel Salazar

item.title: ‘Dangerous, Dirty, Violent, and Young,’ by Zayd Ayers Dohrn
item.description: Zayd Ayers Dohrn’s parents were leaders of the Weather Underground. His new book traces how their revolutionary ideals collided with their family life.
item.author: Dana Spiotta

item.title: ‘Talking Classics,’ by Mary Beard
item.description: For her latest book, the popular British scholar Mary Beard gets personal about how she fell for ancient Greece and Rome.
item.author: Sarah Ruden

item.title: ‘An Inconvenient Widow,’ by Lois Romano
item.description: In “An Inconvenient Widow,” Lois Romano defends the most reviled first lady from her detractors past and present.
item.author: Amy S. Greenberg

item.title: ‘How to Rule the World,’ by Theo Baker
item.description: In his first book, Theo Baker chronicles an outrageously eventful year navigating an enigmatic center of power.
item.author: Anand Giridharadas

item.title: ‘On Witness and Respair,’ by Jesmyn Ward
item.description: The two-time National Book Award winner collects essays, profiles and appreciations in a new book, “On Witness and Respair.”
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Theater,’ by James Verini
item.description: In “The Theater,” the journalist James Verini recounts the bombing of a performing arts space turned refugee shelter in the middle of war-torn Mariupol.
item.author: Caroline Alexander

item.title: ‘The Wonderful World That Almost Was: A Life of Peter Hujar and Paul Thek’
item.description: In “The Wonderful World That Almost Was,” Andrew Durbin reconstructs the coterie that surrounded the artist-lovers Peter Hujar and Paul Thek.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Take Me With You,’ by Steven Rowley
item.description: Steven Rowley’s new novel, “Take Me With You,” asks who we are without our partners.
item.author: TJ Klune

item.title: ‘Attention-Seeking Behavior,’ by Aea Varfis-van Warmelo
item.description: Aea Varfis-van Warmelo’s novel follows a commitment-averse liar who is plagued by visions of violence.
item.author: Justin Taylor

item.title: ‘Take Me to Your Leader,’ by Neil deGrasse Tyson
item.description: The popular astrophysicist can be witty and informative when making science accessible. You wouldn’t know it from his new book, “Take Me to Your Leader.”
item.author: Alec Nevala-Lee

item.title: ‘Seek the Traitor’s Son,’ by Veronica Roth
item.description: In Veronica Roth’s “Seek the Traitor’s Son,” a soldier in a pandemic-ravaged world is forced to become the hope of her people.
item.author: Everdeen Mason

item.title: ‘Nerve Damage,’ by Annakeara Stinson
item.description: “Nerve Damage,” by Annakeara Stinson, is a jittery psychological thriller about a woman whose creepy ex simply won’t leave her alone.
item.author: Violet Kupersmith

item.title: ‘AI for Good,’ by Josh Tyrangiel
item.description: In “AI for Good,” Josh Tyrangiel travels to the classrooms, hospitals and research labs where people are using artificial intelligence that might benefit society.
item.author: Max Chafkin

item.title: ‘Men Like Ours,’ by Bindu Bansinath
item.description: “Men Like Ours,” a novel by Bindu Bansinath, follows an immigrant family through a community crisis.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘When the Forest Breathes,’ by Suzanne Simard
item.description: Suzanne Simard’s new book urges Western science to take a lesson from the more holistic Indigenous approach to forest preservation.
item.author: Deborah Blum

item.title: ‘Look What You Made Me Do,’ by John Lanchester
item.description: In John Lanchester’s “Look What You Made Me Do,” a widow is unnerved when a hit TV series airs details from her marriage a little too closely.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘Selling Opportunity,’ by Mary Lisa Gavenas
item.description: In “Selling Opportunity,” Mary Lisa Gavenas tells the not-always-rosy story of Mary Kay, the brand — and its founder.
item.author: Marisa Meltzer

item.title: ‘One Leg on Earth,’ by ‘Pemi Aguda
item.description: In “One Leg on Earth,” a young college grad’s idealistic move to Lagos turns into a nightmare.
item.author: Ainslie Hogarth

item.title: ‘Screen People,’ by Megan Garber
item.description: In “Screen People,” Megan Garber looks at how we all became famous for 15 minutes.
item.author: Madison Malone Kircher

item.title: ‘Revenge for the Sixties,’ by Peter S. Canellos; ‘Alito,’ by Mollie Hemingway
item.description: Two new biographies of the Supreme Court justice show how his career was propelled by a legal movement that coalesced to take down Roe v. Wade.
item.author: Mary Jo McConahay

item.title: ‘The Last Contract of Isako,’ by Fonda Lee
item.description: Fonda Lee’s “cyberpunk samurai in space” novel follows a sword-wielding warrior trying to finish one last job.
item.author: Amal El-Mohtar

item.title: ‘From Life Itself,’ by Suzy Hansen
item.description: In a new book, the journalist Suzy Hansen plumbs an Istanbul community for insights into Turkey’s hard-right turn.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai
